evaluations.bug_description: The backtracking algorithm can return True before the length of b exceeds n, allowing solutions with more than n elements. For example, with n=1 and a=[5] the algorithm starts with b=[0], adds a candidate 5, obtains b=[0,5] (length 2) and returns YES, even though a valid b of length 1 does not exist (only a=0 is possible). This violates the requirement that the sequence b have exactly n elements.
evaluations.reasoning: The code checks the coverage condition a_set <= diffs before verifying that len(b) == n. Consequently it may accept a solution that uses more than n integers, leading to incorrect YES answers for cases where a solution of length n does not exist. The early return should be conditioned on len(b) <= n, or the length check should be performed before accepting a solution.

bugs.description: The backtracking algorithm may return True before the constructed sequence b reaches the required length n. It checks the coverage condition a_set <= diffs before verifying len(b) == n, allowing it to accept a solution that uses more than n elements. Such a solution does not guarantee the existence of a solution with exactly n elements, leading to incorrect YES answers.
bugs.rebuttal_to: The backtracking algorithm can return True before the length of b exceeds n, allowing solutions with more than n elements. For example, with n=1 and a=[5] the algorithm starts with b=[0], adds a candidate 5, obtains b=[0,5] (length 2) and returns YES, even though a valid b of length 1 does not exist (only a=0 is possible). This violates the requirement that the sequence b have exactly n elements.

bugs.description: The backtracking algorithm may return True before the constructed sequence b reaches the required length n. It checks the coverage condition a_set <= diffs before verifying len(b) == n, allowing it to accept a solution that uses more than n elements. Such a solution does not guarantee the existence of a solution with exactly n elements, leading to incorrect YES answers.
bugs.rebuttal_to: The backtracking algorithm can return True before the length of b exceeds n, allowing solutions with more than n elements. For example, with n=1 and a=[5] the algorithm starts with b=[0], adds a candidate 5, obtains b=[0,5] (length 2) and returns YES, even though a valid b of length 1 does not exist (only a=0 is possible). This violates the requirement that the sequence b have exactly n elements.

bugs.bug_description: The backtracking algorithm may return True before the constructed sequence b reaches the required length n. It checks the coverage condition a_set <= diffs before verifying len(b) == n, allowing it to accept a solution that uses more than n elements. Such a solution does not guarantee the existence of a solution with exactly n elements, leading to incorrect YES answers.
bugs.reasoning: In backtrack(b) the first check is `if a_set <= diffs: return True`. This can succeed when len(b) < n, meaning the algorithm accepts a set of differences that covers all a_i using fewer than n elements in b. The subsequent length check (`if len(b) == n: return False`) is only reached when the coverage condition fails. Consequently the algorithm may return True with a b longer than n (or shorter than n) and never explore the case where exactly n elements are required. A concrete counterexample is n=1, a=[5]: the algorithm starts with b=[0], adds 5 to obtain b=[0,5] (len=2>n) and then returns True because the differences {0,5,-5} cover a. The correct answer should be NO because a single-element b can only produce the difference 0. Hence the bug is real and leads to incorrect YES answers.

evaluations.bug_description: The backtracking algorithm may return True before the constructed sequence b reaches the required length n. It checks the coverage condition a_set <= diffs before verifying len(b) == n, allowing it to accept a solution that uses more than n elements. Such a solution does not guarantee the existence of a solution with exactly n elements, leading to incorrect YES answers.
evaluations.reasoning: In backtrack(b) the first check is `if a_set <= diffs: return True`. This can succeed when len(b) < n, meaning the algorithm accepts a set of differences that covers all a_i using fewer than n elements in b. The subsequent length check (`if len(b) == n: return False`) is only reached when the coverage condition fails. Consequently the algorithm may return True with a b longer than n (or shorter than n) and never explore the case where exactly n elements are required. A concrete counterexample is n=1, a=[5]: the algorithm starts with b=[0], adds 5 to obtain b=[0,5] (len=2>n) and then returns True because the differences {0,5,-5} cover a. The correct answer should be NO because a single-element b can only produce the difference 0. Hence the bug is real and leads to incorrect YES answers.

bugs.description: The backtracking algorithm may return True before the constructed sequence b reaches the required length n. It checks the coverage condition a_set <= diffs before verifying len(b) == n, allowing it to accept a solution that uses more than n elements (or fewer). This can produce incorrect YES answers for cases where no sequence of exactly n integers exists.
bugs.reasoning: The algorithm starts with b=[0] and can add new elements until the set of differences covers all a_i, returning True even if the length of b exceeds n. A concrete counterexample is n=2, a=[1,2]; the algorithm finds b=[0,1,2] (length 3) covering both differences and returns YES, while the correct answer is NO because any b of length 2 cannot produce both 1 and 2 as differences. Therefore the bug is real and leads to incorrect outputs.

final_bugs.description: The backtracking algorithm may return True before the constructed sequence b reaches the required length n. It checks the coverage condition a_set <= diffs before verifying len(b) == n, allowing it to accept a solution that uses more than n elements (or fewer). This can produce incorrect YES answers for cases where no sequence of exactly n integers exists.
final_bugs.reasoning: The algorithm starts with b=[0] and can add new elements until the set of differences covers all a_i, returning True even if the length of b exceeds n. A concrete counterexample is n=2, a=[1,2]; the algorithm finds b=[0,1,2] (length 3) covering both differences and returns YES, while the correct answer is NO because any b of length 2 cannot produce both 1 and 2 as differences. Therefore the bug is real and leads to incorrect outputs.
